trend_name: Margot Robbie Just Cosigned the Pageboy Bob Haircut for Spring
description:
IfBella Hadidhadn’t already convinced us to chop all our hair off for spring, Margot Robbie just made it official. Hadid and Robbie both ditched long length for apageboy bobandwispy fringe—and their different takes on the look prove just how versatile this trending cut can be.
Robbie marked the official end of herWuthering Heightsera—and all thelong, crimped wavesthat came with it—with a dramatic hair transformation. On March 9 she debuted her new cut at the Chanel presentation at Paris Fashion Week: A tousledbobthat hits just above her shoulders, paired with a light, wispy fringe. And while her go-to stylistBryce Scarlettmay have teased the chop with aglamorous Olsen tuck last month, it’s the shortest we’ve seen Robbie go sincepre-Barbie.
While the traditional pageboy bob is a bit shorter (usually skimming the jaw, à la Hadid’s recent chop), Margot Robbie’s version borrows its unstructured shape and soft layering. Modern iterations of the cut favor natural texture variation and subtle layers. In fact,Elliot Bute, a stylist at London salon Hersheson, encourages you to “play with different lengths—from chin-length toshoulder-length—and experiment with bangs, choosing a wavy or side-swept version instead of a traditional blunt fringe.”
WATCH THIS
Love Island's Olandria Carthen Reveals What's On Her Phone
Although Robbie’s ends are kept heavy, her new cut is much lower maintenance than arazor-sharp blunt bob. Her natural-looking waves add movement and texture, while the barely there bangs bring an effortless quality to the look.
Interested in a shorthaircut for springand summer? Our favorite stars are getting this one right. This bob is designed to support beachy texture (and even a little frizz), and wispy bangs are easy to keep out of your face when you can’t be bothered to style them. Speaking of styling: This cut favors the less-is-more approach. According to Bute, a little mousse ortexturizing spraycan go a long way to enhance the shape of the pageboy bob, no matter which length you choose.
Grace McCarty is the associate beauty editor atGlamour.

trend_name: Bella Hadid Wore a Faux ‘Pageboy Bob’ to Welcome in Spring
description:
Bella Hadid just delivered a masterclass in test-driving anew haircutwithout making a permanent change. The 29-year-old model stepped out at Paris Fashion Week with a fauxpageboy bob, which (thanks to her layers andface-framing pieces) made it look as if she’d actually had herlong layerschopped to chin-length.
A representative for Hadid toldGlamourthat her new “bob” was actually the result of some masterful hair tucking. But since the pageboy bob is shaping up to be one of the trendiest haircuts for spring, maybe this road test could lead to the real chop in the future?
Clearly, the 29-year-old model has had an itch for a hair transformation recently. Last week shedebutednew wispy bangs with her sandy,low-maintenance blondecolor.
Although Hadid’s cut may not be the real deal, it reminded us of all the reasons to love the pageboy bob. A cut with ’60s roots, “The traditional pageboy bob features a rounded, ear-to-shoulder length with inward-curled blunt ends and a heavy fringe,”Elliot Bute, a stylist at London salon Hershesons previously toldGlamour.
WATCH THIS
Miss Piggy & Kermit Take a Friendship Test
While OG pageboy bobs (think Princess Diana) had a distinctly rounded shape, modern versions like Hadid’s look “add varied lengths and textured finishes, transforming the once-rigid style into something more versatile,” Bute says.
Unlike ablunt bobwhich favors sharp lines, the softer pageboy bob invites tousled texture. That’s what makes it an effortlessshort haircutfor spring and summer: It’s short enough to keep things cool but not so precise that you’ll need to tote around yourflatiron. While this look can be styled sleek, it looks just as intentional with a hit oftexture sprayand some natural bends.
Feeling inspired by Bella Hadid’s latest look? The good news is there are plenty of ways to customize the pageboy bob to your unique face shape and hair type. Bute highly recommends experimenting with fringe for this cut: Opt for wispywisteria bangsfor something low maintenance or turn up the face-framing flattery with a thickercurtain fringe.
You can also get the essence of the look without committing to a super-short cut. “Play with different lengths—from chin-length toshoulder-length,” Bute recommends. Midlength haircuts more your speed? “Aim for a length that sits around the shoulders and layers that highlight facial features and add movement,” he says.
Grace McCarty is the associate beauty editor atGlamour.

trend_name: Emma Stone’s ‘Soft Swing Bob’ Was the Coolest Spring Haircut at the 2026 Oscars
description:
All products featured on Glamour are independently selected by our editors. However, we may receive compensation from retailers and/or from purchases of products through these links.
After appearing at last year’s Academy Awards with apixie cut, Emma Stone has since grown out her hair into a soft swingbobthat’s perfect for spring. For the2026 Oscars, Stone took home the award forcoolest haircutthanks to this one detail.
Stone, who was nominated for best actress in a leading role for the filmBugonia,wore aBridgerton-coded Louis Vuitton gownwith a romantic hairstyle to match. Within a year, her pixie has grown into a shiny, healthy, and bouncy long bob. Styled by Mara Roszak using GHD hair tools, Stone’s hair was blow-dried and curled slightly in different directions at the ends, creating a beautiful wave that gives it a soft swing.
“For the Oscars, I wanted a 90’s blowout with soft movement in Emma’s bob, keeping it feeling fresh, modern, and full,” Roszak said in a press release. “The look was polished but effortless—soft volume, subtle bend, natural volume, and subtly brushed through.”
WATCH THIS
Hilary Duff Watches Fan Covers Online
Her “soft auburn” hair color was done by colorist Tracey Cunningham who created a custom blend usingSchwarzkopf Professional’s Igora Vibrancedemi-permanent color and Olaplex hair treatmentsNo.3 Plus Complete Repair TreatmentandNo. 1 Bond Multiplier. The result? A vibrant color with plenty of shine and health, giving her hairstyle even more radiance (in addition to her happy smile). Due to the curls, her long bob is slightly shortened so that it ends below her chin and falls softly.
A particularly eye-catching feature of Emma Stone’s hairstyle is the deep side parting, which is definitely on-trend at the moment. One side falls deeper into her face and plays around it, while the other half is tucked behind her ear, which in turn opens up her face and emphasizes her features.
When it comes to makeup, facialistIván Polfirst prepped Stone’s skin usingThe Beauty Sandwich Amuse Bouche. Her spring-ready makeup look featured pink cheeks, champagne-colored eyeshadow, andmascara. For the lips, Stone joined the Oscars trend ofballet slipper lipswith a satin pink lip.
A version of this story first appeared inGlamourGermany.

This clarifying shampoo comes highly recommended byHadley King, MD, a board-certified dermatologist in NYC. She loves that it removes residue and gently exfoliates with the help of salicylic acid, which is oil-soluble and can penetrate into pores ​to gently exfoliate and remove excess sebum. “It’s a nice choice for oily hair because it removes 100% of residue and is very lightweight, so it won’t weigh down strands,” says Dr. King. “And although there’s no residue, it won’t cause adry scalpor strip the hair of moisture because it contains glycerin, aloe vera, and vitamins B3and B6—these ingredients help to support scalp health.” It made it to the top of our list for these reasons, and we also love how affordable it is, at under $10.
If you’re prone to acne on the scalp and/or body areas adjacent to the scalp, King recommends trying a shampoo with salicylic acid, like this one.
Glamourcommerce writerAlanna Martine Kilkearysays:
“It smells super fresh and sweet, and makes me look forward to washing my hair. And if you’re someone who doesn’t feel like they’re getting a deep clean unless they see a lot of bubbles, this shampoo lathers so well,” she says. Kilkeary has naturally oily hair that gets pretty greasy even after a day, but incorporating this into her hair care routine for a week made a real difference. “I didn’t feel that I had towash my hair every daylike usual. Instead, I was able to go two whole days without feeling like a total greaseball. My hair was soft and shiny, and my scalp felt fresh.”
Glamourassociate beauty editorGracy McCartysays:
“When you need a clean slate, this clarifying shampoo from Garnier gets the job done. My fine hair falls flat quickly so I’m constantly hitting it with dry shampoo and texturizers—making a weekly clarifier a non negotiable. This super affordable option from Garnier is a no frills workhorse. It leaves my hair and scalp squeaky clean (literally) without a trace of product buildup. I notice that my hair feels lighter and bouncier, especially in my roots and bangs. It might leave your hair feeling a little stripped, but pairing it with a lightweight hair mask does the trick for me.”
Fast facts:

A deep-cleaning shampoo is imperative to rid hair of product buildup, and this one will do the job without stripping your scalp or leaving it feeling dry or sensitive post-cleanse. It has a botanical blend of prickly pear seed oil, cactus flower extract and moringa leaf extract that helps hydrate hair, keeps flyaways at bay, and strengthens (especially if you have color-treated or damaged strands). It also helps clarify excess oil and sebum buildup, and smells heavenly.
Glamour’sKilkeary says:
“I’m already a huge fan of theDae Dry Shampoo, so I couldn’t wait to put their new detoxclarifying shampooto the test. This one is a dream to apply—it lathers beautifully and gives you a ton of suds. My hair and scalp felt very clean after using this one, and I also noticed a difference in the volume and feel of my hair after I dried it. My hair was not flat or extra shiny like usual; instead, it was quite bouncy and thick. I’d definitely recommend this one if you’re looking for an affordable deep-cleansing shampoo to combat oiliness that feels just incredibly luxe.”
Fast facts:

If you havecurly hair, product buildup due to occlusive hair care products (like leave-in treatments or gels) might be rampant, so it’s important to deep clean your scalp for more balance. We love this shampoo-and-scrub combo that gently exfoliates with willow bark and tea tree oil. It also contains niacinamide to help soften your hair, promote hair growth, and has a refreshing lemon-and-peppermint scent that perks you up every time you use it. This one is a favorite for curly hair types specifically because it does a great job at deep cleaning without leaving your curls dry or frizzy.
GlamourcontributorMonique Wilsonsays:
“The Cécred Clarifying Shampoo & Scalp Scrub is one of my favorite products to use on my hair. The first time I used it, my hair instantly felt clean after the first wash. The combination of exfoliants with the calming nature of tea tree oil is a game-changer in removing weeklong buildup from my curls. Sometimes when I use products, it feels like my hair is being stripped of my natural oils. But the Clarifying Shampoo & Scalp Scrub clarifies my hair without overstripping, and my curls look and feel soft post-wash. Plus, the nozzle applicator makes it so easy to apply the product. Every shampoo product needs it.”
Fast facts:

trend_name: Act + Acre Clarifying Hard Water Shampoo
description:
If you live in an area where hard water is abundant, your hair might need a little extra TLC to rid it of minerals (which can supremely dry it out). This shampoo made our list because it strikes the right balance, leaving your hair looking shiny without feeling greasy or weighed down. It gives hair a deep clean, which is especially useful if you deal with hard water buildup. It combines coconut and fulvic acid to deeply hydrate and remove residue from minerals and hair products, leaving hair healthier-looking.
Glamour’sKilkeary says:
“Not only does my hair get oily at the roots, but it also gets gross and frizzy around the midshaft when I don’t wash it for a day or two. The texture becomes stringy and almost looks wet (TMI, I know), and this is always the case every time I travel, too, when my hair is exposed to areas with mineral-heavy water. This shampoo did such wonders at breathing life back into my hair. After washing and drying, I styled my hair into a bubble pony, and it held a lot of volume and shape. My hair was slicked back at the top but didn’t appear greasy. I’ve continued to use this shampoo since, and overall my hair is a lot softer and stays fresh throughout the day.”
Fast facts:

trend_name: Oribe Serene Scalp Oil Control Shampoo
description:
If you want to control oil on your scalp (read: if it’s red, irritated, itchy or inflamed), we highly recommend this luxe shampoo that blends amino acids, prebiotics, and biome-balancing ingredients to help reduce sebum and oil. The consistency of this sulfate-free shampoo is more luxurious than others on the list—it’s thick and creamy, lathers into a nice layer of suds, and makes hair feel soft and flake-free after washing. It also does a good job of keeping flyaways, frizz, and breakage at bay for a smoother, velvet-like finish at the root, which is especially useful if you like to slick back your hair into ponytails or buns. Post-wash, your scalp will feel comfortable and clean, and you’ll notice a difference in flakes and oiliness.
Glamour’sKilkeary says:
“This shampoo has probably one of the best textures on this list. It’s a gel that foams into a really nice lather and washes out easily for smooth, grease-free strands. I noticed that my scalp was less flaky and oily post-wash, and I’d definitely recommend it for anyone looking to combat oily roots and an unbalanced scalp.”
Fast facts:

Have greasy roots butdry ends?Bradley Leake, a professional hairstylist based in Los Angeles, suggests this balancing shampoo, which he describes as “an ultralight formula that addresses scalp oiliness.” It contains amino acids to reduce excess oil and odors post-wash, while vitamin B6 helps gently nourish lengths to keep them frizz free and smooth. Leake also notes that it’s a great volumizing shampoo for flat hair, which is yet another reason he loves using it with clients. You’re left with a voluminous, silky smooth finish that looks like you just got a blowout.
Glamoursenior commerce editorJenifer Callesays:
“My hair tends to get greasy after a day, but I’ve noticed that after using this Kerastase shampoo, my strands stay shine-free for up to three days. Just keep in mind that it doesn’t lather at all, so I would recommenddouble- (or triple-) washingwith another shampoo. I have coarse, long hair so I do end up using a lot of product, which adds up, but I can honestly say that this is the only hair product that’s actually helped my greasy strands, but also managed to keep the ends of my hair soft and moisturized.”
Fast facts:

trend_name: Briogeo Scalp Revival Charcoal + Coconut Oil Micro-Exfoliating Scalp Scrub Shampoo
description:
This exfoliating microscrub helps clear away dandruff and buildup that can be causing excess grease and oil. It has detoxifying charcoal, which helps break down gunk, plus coconut oil to gently hydrate and soothe. The texture of this shampoo is different from other picks on the list because it has tiny micro-crystals that physically slough away dead skin and flakes that might be clogging up the scalp. We also like that this one features tea tree and peppermint oil to help calm inflammation and itchiness and balance out the scalp.
Glamour’sKilkeary says:
“I’ve been a long-time fan of this dandruff shampoo and scrub, particularly when my scalp is feeling extra congested, and my hair looks oily at the root. It has microfine crystals that physically slough away flakes, and I noticed a huge difference in my dandruff after incorporating this formula into my routine for about two weeks. It’s super thick, and a little goes a long way too—I like to leave it in for about three to five minutes while I condition my ends to get more of a hair-mask effect.”
GlamourcontributorBrigitt Earleysays:
“I have long, wavy hair and have always struggled with a distractingly itchy scalp prone to white flakes from giving into the urge to scratch. After trying this, I think I went two to three whole days before I started itching—I usually can’t go more than a few hours!” She also liked how small the exfoliating particles are. “Other products I tried had big pieces of salt or sugar that were harder to fully work into my scalp.”
Fast facts:

For a no-nonsenseclarifying shampooyou can pick up at a nearby Target or Ulta, we highly recommend this pick from Odele. It has a gel-like, lightweight texture that feels weightless during application, but does a great job of removing product build-up and heavy metals from hard water that sinks into the hair and scalp over time. It contains hydrolyzed rice protein (something you might find in a luxury alternative) to help strengthen hair and prevent breakage, too, for a softer and shinier finish, plus plant seed extracts to help protect and soothe. It’s only $12 too, which makes it one of the most affordable picks on this list, but the experience (it lathers amazingly) makes it feel like a luxury treatment.
Glamour’sKilkeary says:
“Shampoos for oily hair are sometimes packed with drying ingredients to help soak up oil, but they often leave strands high andverydry. This paraben-free shampoo manages the impossible by clearing up oiliness and buildup without making hair feel crunchy or prone to breakage. My hair is always soft, easy to run my fingers through, and flake-free after use.”
Fast facts:

What causes greasy hair in the first place?
“Touching your hair frequently throughout the day can transfer oils from your hands to your hair, causing it to become greasy,” saysDendy Engelman, MD. But if you experience excessive oiliness, there is likely an internal cause. “Hormonal changes, genetics, and certain skin conditions like seborrheic dermatitis or PCOS, and others can all cause the scalp to produce excessive amounts of oil, leading to oily hair,” she says.
What are signs of unhealthy oily hair?
Most people produce a small amount of oil on their scalps, which is completely normal and actually good for your scalp and hair. There are signs of overproduction. “It’s often if your scalp and hair are oily to the point that it’s limp, and can’t hold a style from the oil,” says celebrity hairstylistAlex Pardoe. Another major sign is clogged follicles on the scalp, which can appear as small pimples. “Clogged follicles can hinder your hair from growing, and can cause excess shedding,” he says.
How do you wash oily hair correctly?
By wetting your hair with warm water to open up the cuticles, saysMarisa Garshick, MD, FAAD, a board-certified dermatologist in New York City. Then use a small amount of shampoo and gently massage the scalp with your fingertips or ascalp massagerto lift excess oil and dirt.
Next, rinse hair thoroughly and follow with a lightweight, oil-free conditioner applied only to the ends. It’s also key not tooverwash your hair. “Washing too often, especially with stripping shampoos, can exacerbate the problem and overstrip the natural oils in your hair,” says Dr. Engelman. When this happens, your scalp might compensate by increasing oil production, and you’re back at square one.
When you’re in between wash days,Brendnetta Ashley, a hairstylist and professional ambassador for hair care and color brand Schwarzkopf, recommendsdry shampoo. “Apply it directly to the scalp, massage into the hair, and style as usual. This is a quick fix if you’re running low on time,” she says.
What should you look for in a shampoo for oily hair?
Finding the right shampoo for oily hair comes down to ingredients. “Oily hair types should avoid shampoos that contain heavy oils or silicones, as these will weigh your hair down and add more oil to your hair,” says Ashley. The stylists we spoke with also note to avoid extra-creamy or moisturizing shampoos, hair masks, and heavy hair oils, and instead stick to lightweight, clarifying options.
“Shampooing twice also helps, making sure to really massage and cleanse the scalp. If your hair is on the finer side, I would skip conditioner; if your hair is medium to thick, just use conditioner from the midshaft down,” saysMiguel Luis, a hairstylist at theRita Hazan Salonin NYC.
How often should you wash oily hair?
If you have oily hair, you should be washing it every two days, according toGeorge Papanikolas, celebrity hairstylist and global brand ambassador for professional hair care and color brand Matrix. “Try not to wash daily, as it can make it worse. Use dry shampoo in between washes to prolong your style,” he says.
Are there ways to train your hair to not be greasy?
While you can’t get rid of grease altogether, you can train your hair to be less oily. “Space out your washes slowly, use dry shampoo every day, and avoid touching your scalp too much,” says Papanikolas.
Can certain hair products actually cause your hair to be greasy and produce more oil?
Yes. “Thick oils, rich conditioners, or heavy styling creams applied near the scalp can weigh hair down and trigger your scalp to produce even more oil. Keep oils and leave-in products focused on the midlengths to ends instead,” saysSean Michael, a hairstylist and owner ofSalon Beauin Andover, Massachusetts. He also recommends not applying conditioner directly or near your scalp as this can cause oiliness (conditioner is meant to be used on the midlengths and ends only), and to try not to rely heavily on dry shampoo: “While it’s great in moderation, overuse can clog scalp pores and lead to more oil buildup over time.”
What we looked for when testing
We tested shampoos for oily hair on a variety of hair types, including fine hair, textured hair, and curly hair, to see if they met the following criteria. Our testers used the formulas for a span of two to four weeks and took note of how long it took each formula to garner results. We also made sure that they tested these formulas at least two to three times per week. Then, evaluated each one by taking a closer look at these factors:

trend_name: Hot Tools Pro Artist 24k Gold Barrel Curling Iron
description:
This Pro Artist 24k Gold Curling Iron has a longer barrel for waist-length hair. At six inches (two inches longer than your typical curler) it’s suitable for a broader range of lengths. It also heats up faster to more precise temperatures, so you can better customize your styling session to suit fine or coarse strands. We chose this as our best overall pick because it’s under $100, beginner-friendly, and leaves behind long-lasting, swooping curls, even on the straightest or hard-to-curl-hair.
GlamourcontributorBrigitt Earleywho has wavy hair says:
“This hair curler feels a little heavy in hand, but it’s an OG for good reason,” says Earley. “The clamp is easy to use, and the new extended barrel allows me to wrap more of my very long hair around the wand, so it gives defined curls on the first pass. I also like the expanded heat settings compared to its original curler, which range from 250°F to 480°F, and the eight-inch swivel cord, which gives you plenty of room to maneuver when you’re styling your hair. It gave me long-lasting, thick curls that stayed put with a little texturizing spray.”
Glamourcommerce writerAlanna Martine Kilkearywho has straight, fine hair says:
“This is a no-nonsense curler because it’s easy to use, even if you’re not a fan of clamp-stylers. The longer barrel is perfect for my long hair; it lets me curl every inch without leaving any pieces out or having to make multiple passes. My hair held its curl for over eight hours, an entire Saturday,” saysGlamour’s Kilkeary.

trend_name: Conair Infiniti Pro Tourmaline Ceramic 1-Inch Curling Iron
description:
Conair impressed us with this no-nonsense iron that’s straightforward to use (even if you’ve barely curled your hair before) and available to pick up at your local drugstore. It’s one of the most lightweight irons (at 0.7 lbs) we’ve ever tried, which makes it ideal for maneuvering around your head and doesn’t weigh your arms down. The barrel itself is thick and although it doesn’t feature a clamp, it’s easy to hold with the cool touch tip at the top. The wand itself never gets overly hot, and it has clear-to-read temperature dials that allow you to adjust the temp up to 400°F, but you can also keep it low at a steady 250-300°F if you have a finer hair type. This one is by far the best bang for your buck, giving you long-lasting curls at an accessible price point.
Glamour’s Kilkeary, who has straight, fine hair says:
“I only heated this baby up to 350°F since my hair is on the finer side, but was extremely impressed with how thick and voluminous it made my hair. It’s lightweight, which I love because I hate having to maneuver a heavy iron around my head, and the cool tip, heat-resistant top makes it easy to hold. It did a great job at giving me billowing curls that were frizz-free. My hair’s cuticle felt protected and smooth after using this too, a far cry from its typical flyaway state during the cold winter months. It definitely rivals some of the luxury irons I’ve tried,” saysGlamour’s Kilkeary.

trend_name: T3 Aire 360 Ceramic Air Styler Blowout Kit
description:
If you love the Dyson Airwrap but cringe at the price tag, T3 is the answer. Although it’s about half the price of the Dyson, we found that the Aire 360 works just as well—if not better. In fact, we picked it as ourbest hairstyling tool, period. When you turn it on, the air-powered tool picks the hair up and wraps it around the wand to create blowout-style curls without heat damage. In our evaluations, curls were sleek and shiny, which the brand says is thanks to the CeraGloss ceramic surface of the curlers. Testers toldGlamourthat using this hair tool felt like a luxury experience from start to finish. It comes in a beautiful hexagonal box that opens to reveal a pouch for storage, rather than a bulky, hard-to-store box like the one other similar tools come in. This hair curler itself is lightweight and easy to use.
Glamoursenior commerce editorJenifer Callewho has thick, coarse hair says:
“I have thick, coarse hair, so curling it takes forever, which is why this tool is perfect for quick and easy styling in the morning. Since your hair has to be damp for it to work, I use it after my hair wash day and get gorgeous waves. You can customize to make the curls tighter by using smaller sections. And the curls last. The next day I still wake up with wavy strands. Pro tip: It’s okay to leave it on for 10–15 seconds and not worry about damaging your hair since it’s regulated heat versus intense direct heat,” says Calle.
Glamourcontributor Earley who has wavy hair says:
“Everything from the unboxing to the end style is super sophisticated,” says Earley. “It was super intuitive—you simply use the wand head with the proper arrow, so you can curl both sides away from your face without struggle,” says Earley. “I couldn’t believe that I was able to get such a sleek style on my very long hair in less than 15 minutes.”

If you have longer hair, short barrels can make it difficult to wrap your entire strands around and curl your ends. This Bio Ionic curling iron might just solve this problem with its extra-long barrel (eight inches!), which will allow you to curl your hair from root to tip, even if it hangs well below your shoulders. Our beauty writer loves this curling iron because it makes it easy to curl longer strands quickly and efficiently. We also like that the ceramic barrel helps minimize flyaways and frizz, making it perfect for very dry and damaged hair types that are prone to static, as well.
Glamour’s Kilkeary who has straight, fine hair says:
“I forgot to bring hairspray to a wedding weekend, so it was all up to this long-barrel styler and a travel-sizedry shampooto get me through the five-plus-hour event,” she says. Despite her misgivings, the duo worked, and Kilkeary attributes it to this magic curler. “It truly gave me long-lasting curls all night, even through hours of dancing, and without the need for a drop of hairspray, which was incredibly impressive.”

trend_name: T3 SinglePass Curl X Extra-Long Barrel Curling Iron
description:
This slick ceramic curler won’t leave your hair looking frizzy. The extra-long barrel (6.5 inches) is ideal for longer hair. We like that the ceramic clamp grips hair securely without pulling, and that the barrel holds steady heat to style larger sections of hair at a time. This iron is versatile on a handful of hair types, and heats up to around 450°F which can smooth out coarser and curlier strands. The gentle ceramic barrel, though, helps keep in hair’s moisture to give you an extremely shiny and hydrated hair finish.
Glamour’s Kilkeary who has straight, fine hair says:
After testing countless irons over her nearly seven-year beauty career,Glamour’s Kilkeary still counts this as one of the best. “It’s quite ergonomic and allows you to curl hair from its root to its tip with constant adjusting,” she says. It also gave Kilkeary frizz-free curls that lasted for a full day—an impressive feat for her very straight, typically hard-to-curl hair.The brand also makes a tapered version of this curling wand if you like waves that are looser on the top, tighter on the bottom.

trend_name: GHD Classic Wave Oval Curling Wand
description:
While an oval barrel takes a bit of getting used to, this wand offers a great option for natural, undone waves, adding volume without sacrificing length. GHD’s curling iron has an optional spring clamp and a shutoff feature that kicks in after a half-hour of inactivity. Just note that it’s preset to 365°F, which might be too hot for finer hair (lower temperatures are less damaging to this hair type). We also praise it for its ability to give you volumized, textured curls that last for hours, making it a great pick if you curl your hair everyday and want the finish to last from morning to evening hours.
Glamour’s Calle, who has thick hair says:
“When I finally decided to invest in a high-quality curling iron, I went and bought this GHD one because a content creator with the same hair type as me recommended it. Yes, I was influenced, and I’m so glad I made that purchase because years later, it’s still one of my go-to curlers. Not only do I have thick, coarse hair but I also have very hard-to-curl hair. At most, I end up with a wave by the end of the night. I finally got the bouncy curls I was looking for with this GHD curler and the clamp makes it so easy to use. The curls last forever so you can use it for special events like wedding parties or a glamorous date night. I’ve put all my friends on it,” saysGlamour’s Calle.
GlamourcontributorMeg Adamswho has thick hair says:
“This iron, which comes at the recommendation of dozens of celebrity stylists, was a 10-out-of-10 experience for me,” says Adams. “It’s straightforward to use, delivers even heat, and has a cool and touchable tip so you’re less likely to burn your fingers.” In evaluations, the results were some of the more impressive. “After I styled with the spring clamp, my hair was so shiny and so perfect that a girl stopped me on the subway to ask about my style,” says Adams. “When I used the iron as a wand without the clamp, the results were—unbelievably—even greater. I loved the unfinished quality about the voluminous curls.”

trend_name: Paul Mitchell Pro Tools Express Ion Unclipped 3-in-1 Curling Iron
description:
If you have shorter hair and want something lightweight and thin, this interchangeable option from Paul Mitchell is a smart investment. It comes with three ceramic barrels—a 1-inch rod for loose waves, a 1.25-inch cone for natural-looking curls, and a 0.75-inch cone for tighter ringlets—so you can easily switch up your look. The clamp-free, wrap-and-release design speeds up the styling process and helps minimize damage, while the brand’s far-infrared technology and super-charged negative ions help deliver shiny, frizz-free results. The narrower barrels make it easy to curl short and fine hair types, and it doesn’t get overly hot.
Glamour’s Kilkeary who has straight, fine hair says:
“This tapered curling iron is one of the best I’ve ever tried for shorter hair. I used the superthin barrel to create corkscrew-like curls that completely changed the look and texture of my hair. It added so much volume and stayed put for hours throughout the night. I’d recommend this one if you have hard-to-curl hair like mine—it’s truly a game changer. You can see it clearly in my before-and-after,” saysGlamour’s Kilkeary.

Like theSupersonic—Dyson’s best-selling hair dryer—theDyson Airwrapis expensive. But if you’re looking to invest in something that won’t cause hair damage and truly lives up to the hype, it’s a worthy pick, and also one of the best for curly hair. The first-of-its-kind tool dries and styles simultaneously without burning your hair. To curl, you simply attach one of two included curling wands and dangle your hair over it. The Airwrap sucks it close to the barrel, then expertly wraps your hair around the wand—no need to move your hand. If you want a smoother, sleeker style, opt for the smoothing dryer or one of the three other included brush attachments. Yes, it has an eye-popping price tag, but our editors have beenbigfans of the product since its 2018 launch and say it’s worth the splurge.
Glamoursenior beauty editorAriana Yaptangcowho has thick, straight hair says:
“Not to be dramatic, but my life changed when I tried the first-generation Dyson Airwrap,” saysAriana Yaptangco,Glamour’s senior beauty editor. “At the time I had just gotten curtain bangs, and the interchangeable attachments made it so easy to switch according to my styling needs, delivering salon-level smoothness and blowouts in less than half the time.”
Glamourcommerce editorMalia Griggswho has fine, curly hair says:
In her Airwrap review,Glamourcommerce editorMalia Griggsnotes that while there’s a steep learning curve, she appreciated how effective it was at taming frizz. “It consistently gives me a blowout-level finish without leaving my bathroom and doesn’t saddle me with the kind of heat damage that destroys fine hair like mine. When I factor in everything I’m getting—and how much I’ve saved on professional styling and time—the cost feels absolutely worth it to me,” she says.

A great curling wand set can allow you to customize your curl size and style, and this pick is a great option if you want something versatile and affordable. This one comes with four different barrels, including a 1-inch barrel, a 0.5-inch tapered barrel, a 1.25-inch barrel, and a 1.49-inch curling brush. The barrels are interchangeable and have a ceramic coating to help prevent flyaways, static, and frizz. The variety of sizes give you different types of curls, whether you’re looking for tight coils, natural swoops or loose waves. We also like that it comes with ablow dryer brushattachment that also gives you silky, straight strands.
Glamourcontributor Earley who has wavy hair says:
“I really like that this curling iron doesn’t have a clamp—those always seem to leave weird creases in my hair and fry the ends, so that’s a big plus. I also appreciate that it’s interchangeable. I tend to reach for the standard barrel most often, but I’m intrigued by the bubble and tapered options, too. The inclusion of a blow-dry brush is another nice touch, making it a surprisingly versatile tool. My only gripe is that it only offers two heat settings, and it’s not super clear what temperature each one is,” says Earley.

trend_name: BaBylissPro Nano Titanium Professional Spring Curling Iron
description:
This no-frills curling iron is easy to use. It heats up to 450°F without getting overly hot to the touch, and while it’s suitable for all hair types, we think you can get the most out of this if you’re looking to create loose waves. It’s one of the best curling irons we’ve tried on this list because it gave us a long-lasting finish that didn’t fall for hours. It’s also titanium, which means it gets a little hotter than the ceramic options on this list. Even though this feature makes it more suitable for thicker hair types, we’re happy to report that it didn’t singe or burn our tester’s thinner hair.
Glamour’s Kilkeary who has straight, fine hair says:
“I usually don’t gravitate toward curling irons with clamps. I find that the clamp just gets in the way or is a bit too bulky. I was really impressed with how easy and flexible this one was to use. I was able to twirl my hair onto the wand without much finesse or holding onto it, and I also found it quite ergonomic to release the curl (no sticking or getting caught, which sometimes happens with clamp irons). The titanium heats up quickly but doesn’t make my hair burn or smoke, which is definitely ideal. I’d recommend the BaByliss for anyone, no matter what level curler you are. It’s a good one, and I bet it will last me a while,” saysGlamour’s Kilkeary.

What size barrel should I use on my hair?
It depends on your hair length and the type of curl you want.
What temperature should you set your curling iron to?
The ideal temperature depends on your hair type, says celebrity hairstylistDimitris Giannetos. Here’s what he recommends.
Whatever your setting, always use a heat protectant to help prevent damage.
Are ceramic or titanium curling irons better? What about gold curling irons?
It depends on your hair type. Ceramic curling irons distribute heat evenly for smooth, shiny curls and are gentler on fine or fragile hair, says celebrity hairstylistJoseph Maine. Titanium irons are more durable, heat up faster, and hold higher temperatures, which is ideal for thick or coarse hair that needs more power to style. If you’re prone to frizz or damage, tourmaline ceramic is another great option, since it emits negative ions that help boost shine and reduce static.
Gold curling irons aren’t necessarily better than other options, but they can handle higher heat, making them a solid choice for thick or coarse hair. That said, they don’t distribute heat as evenly as ceramic models.
What type of curling iron is least damaging to hair?
While the best choice depends on your hair, ceramic curling irons are generally the least damaging for most people because they hold lower temperatures. If you’re concerned about breakage, ceramic irons are the best choice, though they may not give you a long-lasting finish, according to stylists.
What kind of curling irons do salons use?
Professional stylists in salons often use metal curling irons because they heat up quickly and reach higher temperatures, which is ideal for efficient styling, says celebrity hairstylist Giannetos. Since pros know how to manage heat safely, they can use these tools without causing damage. For at-home use, he recommends ceramic barrels, which are more forgiving and evenly distribute heat to protect your hair.
How do you get beachy waves with a curling iron?
Giannetos recommends using a large curling iron (about two inches) to create loose bends at the midlengths of your hair, leaving the ends mostly straight. After curling, scrunch your hair, then finish with a texturizing spray for volume and a tousled, undone look. TryBumble and Bumble Surf Sea Salt Sprayfor soft, natural texture without the crunch.
How do you make curls last?
To make curls last longer, use a strong-hold hairspray, likeOribe Free Styler Working Hair Spray, to set your style. It offers a strong hold without leaving hair stiff or greasy. Giannetos also recommends adding a frizz-fighting, hydrating product to maintain smoothness and shine. Try a lightweighthair oilor a moisturizing serum likeGarnier Fructis Hair Filler + Hyaluronic Moisture Repair Serum.
Why do my curls fall flat after curling?
You may be skipping crucial prep and finish steps. As previously mentioned, hairspray and texture sprays are key for holding volume and shape. The stylists also emphasized maintaining a healthy hair routine full of hydrating shampoos, conditioners, andhair masks; well-conditioned hair is less brittle and better able to hold a style.

trend_name: How we chose the best curling irons
description:
What to look for in a curling iron
When shopping for a curling wand, consider the following:
Material:Ceramic barrels evenly distribute heat for smooth, shiny curls with less damage, making it great for fine hair. Titanium heats faster and stays hotter, making it ideal for thick or coarse hair. Tourmaline ceramic helps reduce frizz and boost shine—perfect for damaged or frizz-prone strands.
Heat settings:Match the heat to your hair type. To prevent damage to fine hair, keep the temperature below 300°F. Thick, coarse, or curly hair can handle higher temps between 350°F and 450°F.
Barrel size: The smaller the barrel, the tighter the curl. Sizes from 0.5 to 0.75 inches create ringlets, while 1.5 to 2 inches give loose, beachy waves. Long hair benefits from an extra-large barrel for faster styling.
Attachments:Interchangeable barrels offer versatility, letting you switch from tight curls to soft waves with a single tool.
How we tested
To find the best curling irons, we tapped Glamoureditors and contributors with a wide range of hair types and lengths to test more than two dozen top-rated tools. We evaluated each one based on key factors like barrel material (ceramic, titanium, or tourmaline), temperature range, ease of use, and overall performance. We paid close attention to how each tool handled different hair types, how fast it took to heat up, how long the curls lasted (we wanted at least a full day’s wear), and how the hair looked and felt afterward.
Note:Although we might receive gifted products, our recommendations are based on months of testing and evaluating the very best products. We maintain complete editorial independence in the products we choose to feature, meaning we do not benefit financially from including any product, except for purchases made through our links.
Meet the experts
Curling Iron Comparison Chart

Bright blondes are trending warmer this spring—think sunny, not ashy.  “Buttercreamis a balanced, warm blonde that has a youthful reflection and subtle warmth that is creamy and rich,” says NYC-based celebrity hairstylist and color specialistKelly Novobielski. “It has soft, velvety-golden hues that are beigey and bright.”
“This color works best if you’re already naturally light and in the blonde family,” says hairstylistLauren Grummel. To keep the buttery dimension, she says to ask your stylist for a full highlight with bright pops throughout: “Tone it more neutral or cool to get a few white pieces to really pop.”
LE SSERAFIM Reveal What's On Their Phones

Looking for a brown hair color that keeps things light and airy—without venturing intobronde territory? Laura Elliott, British stylist and educator at haircare specialistsNeäl & Wølfrecommends Hailey Bieber's go-to “milky brunette.” This natural-looking tone “combines both cool and warm tones for a polished yet effortless look, often featuring gentle, blended highlights that add depth and dimension,” Elliott says.
Best yet? It's one of the most versatile hair colors out there. "Milky brunette can be adapted to suit any skin tone; warmer, caramel undertones enhance medium and olive complexions, while cooler, ashier tones complement fairer skin for a naturally radiant finish,” Elliott says.

Warmauburn tonesare a low-risk refresh for brunettes—the ideal spring upgrade. But before hopping on this cool-girl trend, make sure to do your homework: “Reddish huesare infamous for washing out quicker than any shade, which is why I instruct my clients to do at-home glosses and deep conditioning in the shower between salon color sessions,” says celebrity stylistRita Hazan.
Not sure if you want to full-commit to a new tone? Weave some auburn streaks in instead. “Shades of auburn mixed throughout really give the hair some subtle pop,” saysJamila Powell, founder of Miami’s Maggie Rose Salon. “I love anything two-toned because it gives amazing dimension throughout your hair.” She suggests this look for warmer-toned brunettes who are looking for a small change.

The ‘quiet-silver’ technique is the trendiest way to gray-blend this spring, and although it's often shown on blondes likeJennifer Anniston, it also works ondarker bases. Jennifer Garner’s go-to celebrity coloristTracey Cunninghamused Schwarzkopf dyes and expert placement seamlessly diffuse the actor's gray strands into her rich, warm brunette base.

Color-melting was one of winter's biggest dye trends, and it shows no sign of slowing down for spring. This hand-painted technique combines the best parts of highlights and balayage treatements to create a “seamless blend of tones with no harsh lines,” says pro stylistNick Stenson. For a seasonal spin, work some sun-kissed blonde tones and warmer streaks into your custom palette.

Super-light hair doesn't have to be icy‚ as Rosé proved with her strawberry-tinged spin on platinum. To try out the look on your blonde hair at home,Emily Boulin, a hairstylist and Sally Beauty DIY color and styling expert, suggests trying theIon Bright White Creme toner in Strawberry Milk. “It adds a hint of strawberry and rose gold to your blonde shade for those wanting something a little unique and out of the box, while still maintaining a natural look,” she says.
Obsessed with shiny strands for spring and summer? Consider going darker; an espresso base is rich and reflective—a color guaranteed to make the most of a home or salongloss treatment.

Margot Robbie's “Wuthering Heights”hair looks gave us season-defining inspiration, from her romantic crimped waves to her low-maintenanceBrontë blonde. This look incorporates face-framing brightness without bleaching all the way to the root, which makes it super easy to maintain. Ideal for natural blondes and light brunettes who want to incorporate their natural root color, it's the perfect subtle upgrade for spring.

If you prefer to keep your color dark and inky, the 'cherry cola' color trend adds a bit of warmth for spring and summer—while still keeping things sleek. “This color trend features either a violet or a blue base, which gives a sultry look instead of an over-the-top loud red,” says Hairstylist William Whatley, creative education director forScruples Haircare.

Black hairnever looked so good for springtime. If true black feels too intense, hairstylistRossano Ferrettisuggests trying off-black espresso.
“Off-black shades, like deep espresso ordark chocolate, provide a softer alternative to true black,” says Ferretti. “These off-black shades retain the depth and richness of black hair while being less dramatic, making them easier to wear for many people. The result is a luxurious, velvety tone that’s just as striking but with a bit more softness and dimension.”

Michelle Cleveland,celebrity hairstylist, extensions artist, educator, and owner ofHair Addict Salon & Extension Barpredicts that this year's trends “will be all about the warm tones in hair color, and one of my all-time favorite warm hair color trends iscowboy copper,” says
“This color combines the warmth of a soft chestnut brown with the vibrancy of a dose of copper and really works wonders for brightening up most complexions,” she continues. “If you’re thinking about trying this color out, consult with your colorist and let them help you determine if warm tones will work best with your skin tone and eye color, all of which contribute to the overall look.”

Beyoncé led the pack of celebs who dyed their hairTargaryen blondein recent years. The name is, of course, a reference toHouse TargaryenfromGame of Thrones, a family known for their dragons, questionable familial relationships, and icy blonde hair.
“Targaryen blonde is winter white blonde,” says celebrity coloristJenna Perry, who has worked with the likes of Kendall Jenner and Bella Hadid. “It’s as virgin blonde as you can get.”

Moroccanoilglobal celebrity coloristMatt Rezcreated this blended blonde forAddison Raeand names it as one of the biggest spring hair color trends. “It gives early 2000s nostalgic hair color,” he says. “It reminds me of when I started doing hair color over two decades ago. It’s perfect for spring as it has both cool and warm tones like spring weather. It’s not really hot, but not cold either. For me, it fits the season well.”
To get the look, Rez recommends asking for “a high-lift color for the neutral warm blonde hints, married in harmony with beige blonde highlights.” TheMoroccanoil Blonde Perfecting Purple Shampoowill help neutralize overly warm tones and keep oxidation under control.

When it comes to warm hair colors for spring, Rez recommends this mocha-inspired brunette. “I love the subtle contrast in this world of brown,” he says. “There’s a texture to the color that gives it nuanced dimension with depth at the root.”
When requesting at the salon, make sure to tweak to your own skin tone and eye color. “I always say how light or dark you go has everything to do and should correlate with your eye color,” Rez says. “The tones of the color should be complimenting skin tone. Don’t be afraid of warmth.”

This dark brunette gets a springtime hair color twist thanks to the olive undertones. “I love green-hued brunettes with ash tones,” says Rez. “It’s sans red and specially flattering on cool skin tones.”
To get the look, request “a green-based ash brown with a gray-green gloss to cancel out any orange and red hues.” You want to avoid red tones altogether. “Ask your colorist to make you a super-customized glaze for use at home,” adds Rez. “This glaze can be customized with direct dyes. I love using theMoroccanoil Color Depositing Masksto create mine.”

For a luminous springtime hair color, Rez loves this goldenbrondehe created forTate McRae. “It’s so reflective and eye catching,” he says. “I can stare at it for hours. It’s the bold ribbons for me. There is an intricacy to the pops of light, however. It has the perfect balance of dark and light color where both stand out but don’t offset each other in the tonal aspect.”
To nail that reflective shine, “ask your colorist to do a gentle lift so you don’t miss the mark of the perfect bronde color.” Another tip? “Lowlights are everything when it comes to this color,” says Rez. “The internal depth is what makes the exterior lighter ribboned highlights pop. The lowlights should be slightly lighter than the base and root color.”

Hairstylist William Whatley, creative education director forScruples Haircare, describes this blonde as “a throwback to a softer era in Hollywood where the blondes were richer and more golden than they were in recent trends with the ashier, cooler blondes.”
“I feel it’s more of an ’80s-era blonde, but not as vibrant,” he says. “In 2025 this color will feature a little softer golden tones.”
This warmer take oncashmere blondefeatures golden honey highlights throughout. “It’s a lighter, warmer blonde for the blondes that want a soft, glamorous look,” says Whatley.

As the name implies, sangria red calls to mind the color of red wine. “It’s a softer, cooler red,” explains Whatley. According to the expert, the lush burgundy tone will add a “sophisticated, radiant depth” to your hair.

Quiet luxuryis still going strong in 2025—yes, even in hair. “This trend consists of a blend of warm brunette tones all throughout the hair, painted specifically to show light and color,” says Cunningham.
“Ask your colorist for a rich brunette base and a mix of lowlights and highlights around the face and throughout the lower two thirds of the hair,” the expert adds.

Add even more dimension to cozy blonde with a caramel balayage on a deep brown base. “I love this color since it is not too blonde and it incorporates natural color with heavy caramel strands,” says Mildice, who adds that the look works especially nicely on curly hair. “The curls need heavier dimension, meaning you don’t highlight small strands since they will blend too much. The strong stripes of caramel accentuate the curl pattern.”
Ask your colorist for heavy caramel-toned highlights. “Perhaps balayage around the face or hairline,” says Mildice. “This color should be refreshed every 6 to 12 weeks.”

Cunningham created Jennifer Lopez’s stunning golden brown-blonde shade, which she thinks will be a trending shade this spring. “The key to the golden bronde look is to really lighten up hair around the face,” she says, “from the top of the head all the way to the tips of the hair around the face, and then seamlessly blend out the golden color throughout the bottom third of the hair all the way around the head.”
She added theOlaplex No. 3 Hair Perfectorwhen dyeing the base color to repair and strengthen ends before adding highlights.

For a brighter take on red hair, Cunningham recommends a true red dye job, like the color she created for Emma Stone. If you’re not a natural redhead (Stone is a natural blonde), Cunningham says to touch base with your colorist beforehand. “If you don’t have naturally red hair and want to go red, talk with your colorist about the best way to do it—and the best way to maintain it.”
It may take multiple sessions to achieve the shade you want. You also have to factor in maintenance. “Red is one of the hardest colors to maintain, so you’ll need to prepare to see your colorist more frequently for touch-ups and gloss treatments to keep hair looking its best,” she says. “Home maintenance is also key with red hair.”

If you’re brunette but looking to spice up your look, try “teasylights”: a technique colorists use whereby they tease your hair before applying color.
“This is an amazing change for brunettes since most feel they have to add blonde into their hair to create dimensions or a new look,” says Mildice. “By lighting the hair and toning it down to a lighter brunette shade, it creates a soft suede blend. Olive to the deepest skin tones would accentuate this look the best, but any texture works.”

If you’re transitioning from brunette to blonde, this subtle shade is for you. “The base starts with a cool acorn like brown,” says Mildice. “That color sweeps its way down into a subtle blonde. Sometimes less is more, especially if you had blonde in the past and went brown. Combine the two!”
For the same soft color, ask for “a light faded ombré starting with a light brown at the roots fading to soft blonde ends.” To avoid a harsh fade, ask your colorist to add dimension midshaft of your hair to make it gradually blend. “The benefit of this color is its less maintenance so you can go without needing a color refresh for at least eight weeks or more depending on how frequently you cut your hair,” she adds.

You’re going to see butterscotch blonde everywhere this spring, Madison Reed master coloristShvonne PerkinstellsGlamour. “Deep blonde will be trending this season, with butterscotch blonde taking the cake,” she says. “This rich, balanced color allows for a beautiful golden depth instead of an icy pop.”
The best part? It’s so easy to DIY. If you’re starting with a light blonde base and looking to add richness, opt for Madison ReedColor Reviving Gloss in Prosecco. “When golden is present, violet tones are key for keeping color balanced,” she continues. “To maintain the color, choose theColorSolve Shampoo and Conditionerflavor best suited for your personal hair needs and add the Color Depositing Shot in Blonde for toning and vibrancy.”

Temperatures may be rising, but hair color shades are cooling down, saysGregory Patterson, celebrity hairstylist and DIY expert forSally Beauty. “We’re looking at very cool tones, but thegoodkind—think very cool, smoky, oaky ash blonde,” he says, pointing toIon’s 6A Dark Ash Blondeas a prime example of what we’re now calling chardonnay blonde.

Perkins and Patterson also assert that cool, smoky shades of espresso and brown are having their moment this spring. “This smoky, natural brunette is a great option for brunettes looking to cool down their tone and is reminiscent of the mushroom brunette trend but with a fresh splash of sandy tones,” says Perkins. “UseMadison Reed’s Color Reviving Glossin Espresso to achieve this color at home, while using theColor Therapy Mask in Caffefor the ultimate refresh between glosses.”

Since hair colors are cooling down, it’s no surprise we’re seeing more ice-cold platinum. “Hair colors, just like anything else in beauty and fashion, go through trends, and hair colors and styles usually ride next to fashion trends,” Patterson says. “At New York Fashion Week the color palettes are super-icy blues and lavender tones. It was very cool, so rather than brightening for spring, we may see a cool-color resurgence.”

As we said, red hair isn’t going anywhere. According toEmily Boulin, hairstylist and Sally Beauty DIY color and styling expert, “All variations of red are in, but copper seems to be the quickest on the rise when clients are looking for a bigger change.”
One especially popular take on the shade is cinnamon copper, which is “making a heavy wave,” saysPresley Poe, hairstylist and color expert forCosmo Prof, who suggests theSchwarzkopf Vibrance linefor maintaining beautiful, rich, and reflective red hair tones.

For a fresh twist on the copper craze, Perkins suggests apricot bronde, which features a light brown base with apricot golden red tones. “A touch darker than strawberry blonde, this color will beautifully warm up your skin tone and complement a natural makeup look,” she says. “The perfect way to nail this color is with ourRadiant Hair Color Kit in Genova Red, and you can refresh the color at home withColor Therapy Mask in Zuccaand Color Reviving Gloss in Cannella.”

If you prefer your hair on the warm side, don’t worry:Expensive brunetteis very much still in vogue. “Brunettes with a golden undertone are rising in popularity for spring,” Boulin tellsGlamour.“It’s subtle, looks natural, and adds a touch of warmth, creating a soft, flattering dimension that looks like it grew out of one’s head organically.”

Expect to seeallsorts of red this year, from burgundy to auburn and everything in between. “Red hair is trending because not only have celebrities been experimenting in this space, but it’s also a great way for blondes to try something deeper or more pigmented without completely going brunette,” says Boulin. “It’s also a good way for some brunettes to go richer or lighter without having to necessarily go blonder. It’s a nice in-between option, and it’s just a matter of finding the right tone.”

“Less is more this spring with a subtle ombré look,” Perkins tellsGlamour.“It draws inspiration from ’70s style icons and features a solid root with grown-in, sun-kissed color weaved throughout the midlengths and ends.Madison Reed’s Light Works Balayage Highlighting Kitis perfect for achieving this look at home.”

Mahogany glow is yet another iteration of the red hair trend, and it’s perfectly suited for dark hair. “These red-brown accents reflect light beautifully on rich brunette hair,” says celebrity coloristJeremy Tardo. “The tones are especially flattering if you have textured hair with a dark natural base color.” For a similar look, he says, ask your colorist for mahogany pigments to be mixed into your usual brunette color.

Matrix celebrity coloristGeorge Papanikolassayscurls are going to shine this spring, so expect to see lots of layering, curly bangs, and women enhancing their natural curls. One way to do so? Adding bold highlights that draw attention to your natural curl pattern. “Chunky highlightsmake this fun and playful so they don’t disappear into the curls,” he says. “Adding a shadow root keeps this looking modern without a harsh line.”

“This look is fun and head-turning without being too drastic of a change from deeper tones,” says Dark and Lovely celebrity stylistDerick Monroe. If you’re going to go lighter, Monroe stresses the importance of keeping your hair healthy enough to handle color.
“The last thing you want to do is a chemical process on already damaged hair,” he says. “A pro tip is to have a maintenance program to keep your hair healthy, shiny, and moisturized. That could be as simple as adeep-conditioning treatmentat home.”

Nectar blonde is a dreamy, creamy shade that strikes the perfect balance between brightness and believability. It has all theboldness of platinumbut is updated with softer, warmer tones, making it way easier to pull off—and maintain. “Nectar blonde looks luxurious, golden, and warm,” says Smith. “The key here is to lighten the hair’s base color to a dark golden blonde, adding warm vanilla cream highlights through the layers and to the face-frame area.”
To get the shade, Smith suggests asking for your beige or golden-blonde base, with hand-painted highlights that are a little chunkier than you might typically go for. “This color works best for those with medium to fair natural hair, as very dark bases tend to have more orange undertones to fight against,” he says. “Don’t use a purple shampoo with this color. Instead, enhance warmth and richness using products with beige and golden tones, such asEvo Fabuloso Beige.”

“This is perfect for anyone who wants change but doesn’t want to go too far out of their comfort zone,” says Monroe. Keep your hair darker at the roots, and add a little lightness to the ends for some dimension that isn’t overwhelming.

To get curls to really pop, add a variation of warm shades on roots and ends. To get a similar look at home, Monroe recommendsDark & Lovely Fade Resist in Chestnut Brownat the roots andHoney Blondeon ends. Since both colors are on the warm side, the result doesn’t look fake, just super dimensional and pretty.

trend_name: What should you consider before getting a bob for thin hair?
description:
Before deciding on the cut, a hairstylist will take into account your hair texture and the density of your strands. “Fine hair can collapse easily, so the cut needs to work with your natural texture,” says Warren. Then ask yourself honestly how much styling you’re willing to do—if you’re a wash-and-go type of girl, then a bendy bob will likely feel out of your comfort zone because it requires a little more effort to craft.
“In terms of length, anything from the middle of the neck up to the jawline works beautifully on thin hair,” Warren says. “Keeping the length shorter helps to maintain this blunt baseline. I tend to avoid longer lob lengths on very fine hair, as they can sometimes fall flat and feel a little limp. If you do go slightly longer, maintaining a strong outline is essential.”

trend_name: What are the best bobs for thin hair?
description:
When it comes to the best bobs for thin hair, a hairstylist’s golden rule is to maintain a blunt silhouette at the ends, as this immediately makes hair look thicker, before leaning into your texture.
If you have straight, fine hair:
“For straight, fine hair, I love a blunt jaw-length bob,” says Warren. “Keeping the outline heavy and clean creates the illusion of density at the ends. You don’t want too much internal layering, as that can make the hair appear wispy. A subtle, invisible layer to encourage movement is enough—the focus should really be on that strong perimeter.”
This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
If you have curly or textured fine hair:
“A slightly softer bob that still maintains a weighty base works best on wavy or curly fine hair,” adds Warren. “Texture naturally adds volume, so it’s about shaping the cut to enhance that while keeping fullness through the bottom. Diffusing can help encourage lift at the root, while the heavier baseline keeps it looking thick rather than fluffy.”
This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.

trend_name: How should you style a bob if you have thin hair?
description:
Follow our step-by-step guide to create maximum volume with minimal effort.
Step 1: Use a volumizing shampoo and conditioner.
Creating a good base to build on starts with the products you use in the shower. Look for lightweightshampoos and conditioners for fine or thinning hairand the wordsvolumizingandbody buildingon the bottle. Avoid putting conditioner on the scalp, as it can make roots look limp and oily.

trend_name: BosleyMD Bos Revive Volumizing Conditioner for Non Color-Treated Hair
description:
Step 2: Apply a texture spray
“On damp hair, apply a lightweight texture spray or volumizing foam, focusing on the mid-lengths and ends,” Warren says.

Step 3: Dry your hair, according to preference.
“Either air-dry with the hair tucked behind the ears to encourage natural shape around the face, or rough-dry by blow-drying hair from left to right and right to left,” says Warren. “If your hair has a natural wave, diffuse upside down to build volume at the root.”

Step 4: Use a curling iron on straight hair.
If your hair is very straight, add a loose bend with acurling ironorstraightener. “Move it in alternating directions—you just want movement, not polished curls,” says Warren.

trend_name: Silk’n SilkyStraight 2-in-1 Hair Straightener and Curling Iron
description:
Step 5: Work a dry texture spray through lengths.
“Once dry, use a product likeLiving Proof Dry Volume & Texture Spraythrough the ends to separate and expand the shape,” says Warren. “Lightly shake the roots out with your fingers to break it up.”

trend_name: Color Wow Style on Steroids Texture+Finishing Spray
description:
Trademark Beauty
Step 6: Finish with a lightweight styling cream.
“Warm it between your hands and just skim the cream over the surface of your hair,” says Warren. “This keeps it polished without flattening the volume you’ve created.”

trend_name: What are the best styling tips for creating maximum volume?
description:
Lots of layers don’t automatically give limp hair an instant boost. “It’s a common mistake—keeping things simple and structured is what makes a bob feel modern and thick,” says Warren.
Avoiding heavy product is also key when styling a bob; applying a whipped mousse from root to tip, or a very fine veil of texture spray, before blow-drying is more effective at encouraging fine hair to hold a voluminous style for longer. Ahair oilalso isn’t a great fit for fine hair as it will weigh the hair down too much and make it look thinner. “Something likeHershesons Zhoosh Foamis perfect to prep the hair before styling or blow-drying because it’ll give the hair texture and grit, and help to create volume at the roots,” says Warren.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
Working with – rather than against – your natural texture, the soft bob maintains a full-bodied perimeter.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
Chin length and with blunt-ish ends, theFrench bobgives fine hair natural movement and texture.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
Cut one length with a precise, straight edge, the blunt bob creates the illusion of fullness by drawing the eye to the thick baseline.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
The beauty of anItalian bob like Lily Collins’son thin hair is that it doesn’t require a whole lot of volume to look good. By sitting neatly against the head and closely cupping the face, it makes a chic feature of hair that has a tendency to fall flat.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
A bendy bob often combines the best of a grown-out pixie cut with a chin-length bob. Crafted into undone bends, the style disguises flyaways and limp ends.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
A fringe adds extra interest to a blunt bob and wispy bangs are having a real moment right now.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
As the name suggests, the nape bob hits the nape of your neck from the back and your ear lobes from the front. The natural movement of a short, blunt cut like this makes a bob feel chunkier on fine hair.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
By scraping hair away from the face and gathering it at the back of the head, a slick wet-look bob is a chic way to disguise thinning hair.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
A graduated bob is shorter in the back and longer in the front for those who prefer more volume on the crown.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
The A-line shape of a jaw-skimming bob ensures the ends look voluminous.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
The super-short cut, mushroom-like shape and root lift are defining features of a micro bob.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
Jaw-skimming or shorter, this is a straight-line bob where the edges have been ever so slightly softened with a razor for an undone finish.

This content can also be viewed on the site itoriginatesfrom.
The subtle, soft layers of thepageboy bobintroduce volume and movement and can be worn wavy or with a side part.
This story originally appeared inGlamourUK.

Preppy style has been on the mood board for years at this point, but it’s starting to loosen up. The new silhouette is free-flowing and ideal for women on the move; nothing’s too tight or stiff to weigh you down on your morning commute or afternoon errands. Consider Chanel’s boxy suits, Celine’s gold-buttoned blazers, and Miu Miu’s V-neck sweaters overbutton-down shirts: It all moves from the office to wherever you’re going after. Tie a silk scarf around your neck or waist to complete the look.
Styling tip:Acing prep is all about the details—you want these pieces to look lived-in. Try fastening a brooch to your blazer, cuffing your jeans, or rolling up your button-down’s sleeves to really sell the look.

trend_name: Abercrombie & Fitch High-Rise ’90s Relaxed Frayed Jeans
description: Abercrombie

After multiple seasons defined by earthy, quiet-luxury-coded hues like chocolate brown and deep burgundy, it’s high time for some fresh energy. Thank Loewe, Dior, Ralph Lauren, and Versace for supplying spring’s in-your-face inspiration. Supersaturated primary and secondary shades popped up practically everywhere we looked this season, bringing to mind a kindergarten classroom’s bright, welcoming color scheme. You’ll be surprised how easy they are to wear.
Styling tip:Go bold by styling these pieces together, or take a more practical route by mixing them into a palette of khaki, black, white, and navy.

trend_name: Polo Ralph Lauren Classic-Fit Linen Shirt
description: Ralph Lauren

Oversized trenches, undone poplin shirts, loose tailoring—so many spring 2026 runways featured models in ensembles seemingly cribbed from someone else’s (a lover’s, perhaps?) wardrobe. The look is relaxed and intimate but never ill-fitting. And you can get the same Chanel- or Bottega-esque look right now, for a lot less money. Why not try a collared shirt thrown over a skirt or a pair of creased trousers thatjustkiss the floor?
Styling tip:Deliberately oversized pieces can be tough to get right, but as long as the important measurements—shoulders for tops, waists for bottoms—are correct, you’ll look purposeful instead of sloppy. Reintroduce some structure by tucking in your shirt or mixing in a more fitted piece.
Abercrombie & Fitch

Forget pastels and ditzy prints—spring 2026’s florals, as demonstrated by the likes of Chloé, Dries Van Noten, Celine, Etro, and Balenciaga, aren’t designed to fade into the background. Instead, whether they’re the entire outfit or just a piece of it, these in-your-face patterns are the focal point, full stop. Ease into the look with a springy top or a maximalist skirt; go full send with a hypnotic dress or a dramatic coat.
Styling tip:Take a cue from the runway looks above and pair your bold florals with relatively subdued accompaniments, likeblack jacketsand brown bags.
Abercrombie
Anthropologie

By now you’ve probably seen the growing demand for track pants, quarter-zips, and funnel-neck jackets firsthand; everyone from J.Crew to Lululemon has a take on each silhouette this spring. But zoom out and you’ll notice a larger pattern, one in whichbody-skimming leggingsand gym-ready tops are giving way to looser, more-elevated silhouettes. These aren’t the track jackets oroversized sweatsyou wore in high school. Instead, labels like Tibi, Tory Burch, Birrot, Zankov, and Kallmeyer are loosening athleisure’s grip on the body—and making every resulting outfit that much cooler.
Styling tip:Mix these loose, active-minded silhouettes with tailored pieces, like evening jackets or pleated trousers, to push your style to the next level.
Net-a-Porter

While spring is often associated with bright colors and loud prints, a surprising number of labels swerved in the opposite direction this season. Cosigned by Tory Burch, Saint Laurent, and quite a few other designers, color-blocked black-and-white ensembles bring a refined elegance that works especially well for office ensembles and event dressing. Plus, while the look may be especially trendy right now, it’s so classic that it’ll never go out of style. Might as well invest in a few favorites to slip into your forever collection.
Styling tip:If such a stark color palette is feeling a little drab, try adding a pop of color—say, tomato red, icy blue, or rich purple—through shoes, bags, or jewelry.

Carrying echoes of military uniforms and workwear, several design details—breast pockets, field jackets, a palette of greens and browns—popped up everywhere from Prada to Victoria Beckham this spring. It’s a reminder that we have the right to ask more from our clothes. Why shouldn’t we have a place for our keys? And why can’t a jacket be rain-resistant? Plus, in the context of, say, amidiskirtor an office layer, cargo pockets look downright chic.
Styling tip:Keep things from getting too utilitarian with smart, chic additions:breezy sandals, statement earrings, pops of feminine color, slouchy bags, and kitten heels.
Gap Factory

A warm breeze flowed through some of our favorite spring collections, reminding us that sunnier days are, in fact, on the way. You could feel it in the flowy, gauzy fabrics and the silhouettes that cascaded off the body, all bringing to mind the way we’d all like to dress on vacation. They also largely shared a common color palette of inky black, crisp white, and dusty earth tones like clay and olive. You don’t need to be packing a carry-on to bring the same energy to your spring looks; just pick up a few of the finds below.
Styling tip:These looks can lean minimalist and might need a little zhuzhing to feel complete. We suggest adding a simple pendant necklace (one of the season’s biggestjewelry trends) to your dresses, vests, and tees to introduce some texture.
Favorite Daughter
